reasoning: The informal statement asks to show that T_inf does not need to be a topology, i.e., ∃ X such that T_inf fails to satisfy the topology axioms (contains univ, closed under arbitrary unions, closed under pairwise intersections). The corrected formalization captures this directly by negating the conjunction of the three topology axioms. The original formalization instead states ¬∀ X, ∀ s ⊆ T_inf, ⋃₀ s ∈ T_inf, which is equivalent to ∃ X, ∃ s ⊆ T_inf, ⋃₀ s ∉ T_inf — i.e., it specifically asserts that T_inf is not closed under arbitrary unions. Logically, this implies the informal claim (if union closure fails, then some axiom fails), but the informal claim does not logically imply this (in principle some other axiom could fail). Hence the original is strictly stronger than the informal/corrected statement. While mathematically these happen to coincide (for T_inf, univ is always in T_inf and intersections are always closed, so union closure is the only possible failure mode), at the level of formal statements they are not logically equivalent. The original is provably true (e.g., X = ℤ with union of singletons {n}, n ≠ 0 yields complement {0} which is finite, nonempty, and not all of X). The error is a misunderstanding of what the informal statement is asking — the formalizer substituted a specific axiom failure (union closure) in place of the generic "is not a topology."
